metadata.summary: pytest fixtures provide reusable setup/teardown via dependency injection. Covers scopes (function/class/module/session), yield cleanup, factory pattern, composition, and parametrized fixtures.

content.title: pytest Fixtures — Scopes, Yield, Factory, and Composition
content.summary: pytest fixtures are functions decorated with @pytest.fixture that provide reusable setup and teardown logic through dependency injection. They eliminate setUp/tearDown boilerplate and allow fine-grained resource lifecycle control via scopes and yield.

why.p: Unlike unittest's setUp/tearDown, pytest fixtures are composable — a fixture can declare other fixtures as parameters, forming a dependency graph that pytest resolves automatically. Scopes let you control how often the setup runs: per-test (function), per-class, per-module, or once per session. The yield pattern integrates teardown directly in the fixture body, keeping setup and cleanup co-located. The factory fixture pattern enables flexible data creation without duplicating logic across tests.

list.item: Any test that needs shared setup — database connections, API clients, sample data.
Tests where teardown matters — closing connections, cleaning temp files, flushing caches.
Tests needing flexible data — factory fixtures allow per-test customization without repetition.
Expensive setup that can be shared — session-scoped fixtures run once for the whole test suite.
Composing complex test scenarios from smaller reusable pieces.
One-off setup that is only used in a single test — inline setup is clearer.
autouse=True for fixtures with DB or HTTP side effects — applies to ALL tests in scope, causing surprises.
Sharing mutable state between tests via a wide-scoped fixture — use function scope or reset the state explicitly.
Fixtures that depend on test execution order — pytest-randomly will expose the dependency as a flake.

section.name: fixture-scopes
section.p: Scope controls when the fixture is instantiated and torn down. The available scopes in ascending lifetime order:

list.item: function (default): new instance per test function.
class: shared across all methods in a test class.
module: shared across all tests in the file.
package: shared across all tests in the package directory.
session: single instance for the entire test run.

rule.statement: Use the smallest fixture scope that keeps test execution time acceptable. Prefer function scope unless a measured performance cost demands widening. A function-scope fixture that takes 50 ms is acceptable; one that takes 3 seconds per test is a candidate for module or session scope.
rule.rationale: Wider scopes share state between tests. If any test mutates shared state and another test relies on a clean state, a random execution order (pytest-randomly) will expose this as a flake. Function scope is the safe default.

rule.statement: A wider-scope fixture MUST NOT depend on a narrower-scope fixture. A session-scoped fixture cannot take a function-scoped fixture as a parameter — pytest raises a ScopeMismatch error at collection time.
rule.rationale: A session fixture lives longer than a function fixture; requesting the shorter-lived resource in the longer-lived context is a lifecycle contract violation.

section.name: yield-teardown
section.p: The yield keyword in a fixture separates setup (before yield) from teardown (after yield). This keeps related code co-located and ensures teardown runs even when a test fails.

example.title: Fixture with yield teardown
example.note: Cleanup after yield runs even if the test raises an exception.

antipattern.description: Returning from a fixture instead of yielding when teardown is needed.
antipattern.reason: If you return rather than yield, there is no place to put teardown code. Resources leak when tests fail.

section.name: factory-pattern
section.p: A factory fixture returns a callable instead of a concrete object. The test calls the factory with custom arguments, getting a tailored instance. This avoids creating N near-identical fixtures for N slightly different objects.

example.title: Factory fixture for flexible user creation

section.name: fixture-composition
section.p: Fixtures compose by declaring other fixtures as parameters. pytest resolves the dependency graph and provides each fixture at the correct scope. Composition replaces inheritance for test setup.

example.title: Composed fixtures for user and team scenarios

section.name: session-scoped-fixtures
section.p: Session-scoped fixtures run once for the entire test suite. Use them for expensive, read-only setup like running migrations or building a compiled artifact. Pair with a function-scoped session/connection fixture that rolls back after each test to maintain test isolation.

example.title: Session-scoped engine + function-scoped session with rollback
example.code:
@pytest.fixture(scope="session") def database_engine(): """Create database engine once for all tests.""" engine = create_engine(TEST_DATABASE_URL) run_migrations(engine) yield engine engine.dispose()
@pytest.fixture(scope="function") def db_session(database_engine): """Fresh database session for each test with rollback.""" connection = database_engine.connect() transaction = connection.begin() session = Session(bind=connection) yield session session.close() transaction.rollback() connection.close()
example.note: The session-scoped engine is shared; the function-scoped session guarantees isolation via rollback.

section.name: parametrized-fixtures
section.p: A fixture decorated with params runs once per param value. Every test that uses the fixture is automatically executed for each param. This differs from @pytest.mark.parametrize — it parametrizes at the fixture level rather than the test level.

example.title: Parametrized database backend fixture
example.code:
@pytest.fixture(params=["sqlite", "postgresql"]) def database(request): """Run tests against multiple database backends.""" db_type = request.param db = create_database(db_type) yield db db.cleanup()
def test_query_performance(database): # Runs twice: once for sqlite, once for postgresql result = database.execute("SELECT * FROM users") assert len(result) >= 0

checklist.check: Choose the smallest scope that keeps test execution time acceptable.
Use yield for any fixture that needs cleanup (connection, file, cache).
Keep fixtures focused and single-purpose — one resource per fixture.
Use factory pattern when tests need slightly different variants of the same object.
Place shared fixtures in conftest.py; domain-specific fixtures in separate files.
Avoid autouse=True for fixtures with side effects (DB writes, HTTP calls).
Verify no test depends on execution order — run with pytest-randomly.
Clean up resources in teardown (after yield), not in a separate test teardown.

list.item: Returning from a fixture instead of yielding when teardown is needed — resources leak on test failure.
Fixture scope mismatch: a function-scope fixture depending on a session-scope fixture works; the reverse raises ScopeMismatch at collection time.
Using autouse=True on a fixture that writes to the database — applies to every test in the directory, including unit tests that never expect DB access.
tmp_path is per-test (function scope); tmp_path_factory is session-scoped — agents pick the wrong one for cache fixtures.
Module globals as shared state — pytest-randomly will expose this as inter-test dependencies.
